panels.text: The forge-city of Ashmark never sleeps. Its furnaces burn through every hour of the night, casting orange light across cobblestones slick with soot and rain.

panels.narration: In the lowest district, a single forge burns apart from the rest — surrounded by silence.
A cloaked figure steps into the light, face hidden.
panels.speaker: Stranger
panels.dialogue: They say you can forge weapons from memories. I want to know if that's true.

panels.narration: Kaden doesn't look up from the anvil.
panels.speaker: Kaden
panels.dialogue: Who told you? And what memory are you offering?

panels.narration: The stranger removes her hood. She is young, perhaps twenty. Her eyes hold something ancient.
panels.speaker: Stranger
panels.dialogue: My most painful one. A night I can never undo. I want it gone — and I want a blade in its place.

panels.text: Kaden sets down his hammer slowly. In thirty years of smithing, he has only attempted memory-forging twice. Both times, the results were catastrophic.

panels.narration: He studies her face — and feels something cold slide down his spine.
panels.speaker: Kaden
panels.dialogue: ...I know you. I don't know from where. But I know you.

panels.text: The stranger smiles. It doesn't reach her eyes. 'That,' she says softly, 'is exactly the problem.'
Memory-forging requires three things: a willing donor, a clear image of grief, and a smith with no fear of what he might see.

panels.narration: Kaden pours a cup of cold tea, more out of habit than thirst.
panels.speaker: Kaden
panels.dialogue: Before I agree to anything — tell me the shape of it. Not the memory itself. Just its shape.

panels.narration: The stranger sits across the fire. She does not blink.
panels.speaker: Stranger
panels.dialogue: A house. A locked door. A name I called out and no one answered.

panels.speaker: Kaden
panels.dialogue: That's loss, not pain. They're different metals.

panels.narration: Something shifts in her expression — just for a moment.
panels.speaker: Stranger
panels.dialogue: Then I'll give you the rest. A fire. Smoke so thick I couldn't see my hands. And the reason I didn't go back in.

panels.text: The fire between them crackles. Kaden stares into it for a long moment.

panels.speaker: Kaden
panels.dialogue: That's guilt. Guilt forges the sharpest blades. And the most dangerous ones.
panels.narration: He stands, moving to the forge.

panels.speaker: Kaden
panels.dialogue: I'll need your name.

panels.speaker: Stranger
panels.dialogue: Vael.
panels.narration: A pause.

panels.text: Kaden's hand freezes over the coals. That name. He knows that name. He carved it once — into a headstone, seven years ago, in the rain.
It starts the night Sera turns seventeen. A thread, thin as spider silk and bright as the moon, wrapped three times around her left wrist.

panels.narration: She sits up in bed and stares at it. It doesn't hurt. It doesn't feel like anything at all.
Her roommate Mika rolls over and squints.
panels.speaker: Mika
panels.dialogue: Sera? Why are you up at 3am holding your own wrist?

panels.speaker: Sera
panels.dialogue: Do you see this? The thread?

panels.speaker: Mika
panels.dialogue: ...I see you pointing at your own arm. Go to sleep.
panels.narration: Mika pulls her blanket over her head.

panels.text: Sera watches the thread all night. By dawn, it has moved — pulling slightly north, toward the edge of town.

panels.narration: She follows it after school. Down familiar streets, then unfamiliar ones.
panels.speaker: Sera
panels.dialogue: (to herself) This is how people end up on the news...

panels.narration: The thread leads her to a rooftop garden above an abandoned print shop. And to a boy sitting on a rusted bench, staring at something on his own wrist.
He looks up. His eyes widen.
panels.speaker: Boy
panels.dialogue: You can see yours too.

panels.text: It isn't a question.

panels.narration: The boy's name is Ren. He has known about the threads since he was twelve.
panels.speaker: Ren
panels.dialogue: I can see everyone's. Not just my own. Yours is... unusually bright.

panels.speaker: Sera
panels.dialogue: What does that mean?

panels.speaker: Ren
panels.dialogue: I'm not sure. Most threads are faint — like veins you can barely see. Yours looks like it's lit from inside.
panels.narration: He hesitates, looking at the thread between them.

panels.text: Sera notices it then. Their threads are connected — a single line from her wrist to his.

panels.speaker: Sera
panels.dialogue: Is that normal?

panels.speaker: Ren
panels.dialogue: No. I have never seen that before.
panels.narration: He says it too carefully.

panels.speaker: Sera
panels.dialogue: You're hiding something.

panels.text: A long pause. The city hums below them.

panels.speaker: Ren
panels.dialogue: When two threads connect like this, they share a timer. When it reaches zero — one of them disappears.

panels.speaker: Sera
panels.dialogue: Disappears. As in...?

panels.speaker: Ren
panels.dialogue: I don't know. No one I've seen come back.
panels.narration: He finally looks at her.

panels.text: Sera looks down at the thread — still bright, still warm. Already a part of her, though she met him five minutes ago.
The Ranking Ceremony happens on your fifth birthday. A needle, a drop of blood, a number burned into your skin before you're old enough to understand what it means.

panels.narration: Yara remembers hers clearly. The machine humming. The technician's face going still. The long silence.
The technician called in a supervisor. Then another. They spoke in low voices.
panels.speaker: Technician
panels.dialogue: The scanner's broken. Run it again.

panels.text: It wasn't broken. The result was always the same: Null. No rank. No classification. An error the system had no category for.
Fourteen years later, Yara has learned to make herself invisible. No rank means no job, no housing allocation, no legal existence. But it also means no record.

panels.narration: She is picking a lock in a government archive when a woman in a black uniform steps out of the shadows.
panels.speaker: Woman
panels.dialogue: Yara. Null designation. We've been looking for you for three years.

panels.speaker: Yara
panels.dialogue: I'm hard to find.

panels.speaker: Woman
panels.dialogue: That's exactly why we need you.

panels.text: The imperial palace has one hundred and twelve kitchens. Lian has memorized every one — not their locations, but their poisons.

panels.narration: She sits at the head taster's table, a bowl of broth in front of her. Three court officials watch from behind a screen.
The senior official's voice is flat.
panels.speaker: Official
panels.dialogue: The position is simple. You eat first. If you survive, the Emperor eats. If you don't — well. That tells us something too.

panels.narration: Lian picks up the spoon.
panels.speaker: Lian
panels.dialogue: What's the mortality rate for this position?

panels.speaker: Official
panels.dialogue: High enough that we stopped keeping records.

panels.text: Lian sips the broth. Notes the faint bitterness under the salt — reedbloom extract, most likely, mixed in just before serving. A delayed toxin. Meant to look like natural causes.
She has been taking reedbloom in small doses since she was nine. Her body metabolizes it like water.

panels.speaker: Lian
panels.dialogue: Delicious.
panels.narration: She sets down the spoon.

panels.text: The officials exchange a glance. They have already decided she is disposable. They have no idea what they have just let inside the palace walls.
The last thing Dax remembers before the impact: the rev of his engine at 280 kph, the rain, and the headlights of a car that shouldn't have been there.

panels.narration: He wakes up three weeks later in a hospital room that smells like antiseptic and unspoken apologies.
The doctor doesn't waste time.
panels.speaker: Doctor
panels.dialogue: The damage to the nerves in your right hand is permanent. You'll have limited grip. Sensitivity is gone in the first two fingers.

panels.speaker: Dax
panels.dialogue: My car.

panels.speaker: Doctor
panels.dialogue: ...I'm sorry?

panels.speaker: Dax
panels.dialogue: What happened to my car.
panels.narration: His voice is flat. Controlled.

panels.text: The doctor leaves. His crew doesn't visit. His phone has forty-three unread messages and he reads none of them.
He spends the next two months relearning how to hold a pen. Then a wrench. Then, finally, a wheel.

panels.narration: His old mechanic, Priya, finds him in a junkyard at midnight, sitting in the shell of a gutted chassis.
panels.speaker: Priya
panels.dialogue: You're going to hurt yourself worse.

panels.speaker: Dax
panels.dialogue: Good. That means it still works.

panels.text: The Glass Throne of Vel was created by the first king as a safeguard — a seat that would shatter beneath any ruler who lied. He died on it within the hour.
Since then, it has sat empty. Aspirants touch it at their coronation. Most walk away bleeding. Some don't walk away at all.

panels.narration: Lady Mireille stands before it alone. She was not supposed to be here — she slipped away from her assigned seat in the gallery.

panels.text: She removes her glove. Presses her bare palm flat against the armrest.
Nothing. No blood. No crack. The throne hums faintly — as if recognizing her.

panels.narration: A voice from the doorway. Lord Casien, the King's Advisor, stands in the shadow.
panels.speaker: Casien
panels.dialogue: Well. That is unexpected.

panels.speaker: Mireille
panels.dialogue: You were watching.

panels.speaker: Casien
panels.dialogue: I watch everything in this palace. Put your glove back on. If the court sees this before I understand what it means — both of us are in danger.

panels.text: Mireille looks at her unmarked hand. She has spent twenty years carefully cultivating the image of someone with nothing to hide. Maybe she was too good at it.
Detective Noa keeps a journal. Has since she was sixteen. Private, handwritten, never photographed. The kind of record that only exists in one place.

panels.narration: The victim is found on a Tuesday. Mid-forties, no signs of struggle, window locked from inside. His last message reads: I already said goodbye to everyone who mattered.

panels.text: Noa notes the phrase. Moves on. Files it as unremarkable.
The second victim is found nine days later. Same cause. Same type of final message. Word for word identical.

panels.narration: Her partner, Dresh, taps the two printouts side by side.
panels.speaker: Dresh
panels.dialogue: Copycat? Or they knew each other?

panels.text: Noa shakes her head slowly. Her throat feels tight. She has read those words before — not in a case file.
She goes home and opens her journal to the entry dated three years ago. There it is. Her own handwriting. I already said goodbye to everyone who mattered.
She had written that on the worst night of her life. She had never shown it to anyone.

chapters.title: A Roof, A Heir, and a Headache

panels.text: Bram had been retired for exactly eleven days when the ceiling collapsed.

panels.narration: To be fair, it was less a collapse and more a directed impact — something small and fast had come through the roof at high speed. That something was currently groaning in a pile of plaster.
Bram stepped over the debris with the weary resignation of a man who had survived seventeen sieges.
panels.speaker: Bram
panels.dialogue: You alive?

panels.narration: The pile of plaster sat up. Beneath it: a child, roughly twelve, with a split lip and the bearing of someone who expected people to bow.
panels.speaker: Child
panels.dialogue: I am Elara Voss, Third Heir to the Cinderstone Throne, and I require your assistance.

panels.speaker: Bram
panels.dialogue: You require a doctor and a mason.

panels.speaker: Elara
panels.dialogue: There are four assassins approximately two minutes behind me.

panels.text: Bram looked at his ceiling. Then at the child. Then at his very comfortable armchair, which he had specifically purchased for retirement.

panels.speaker: Bram
panels.dialogue: ...How did you find this place?

panels.speaker: Elara
panels.dialogue: You are listed in the Imperial Registry as 'Brannick, Deceased.' I assumed that meant you were hiding.

panels.speaker: Bram
panels.dialogue: I was hiding from things like this.
panels.narration: He reaches for his sword, which he had also specifically hung up for retirement.

panels.text: Ji-ho has a rule: never look at a living person's hands.
On the dead, the emotions are fixed — grief, fear, relief, regret. She learned them all by the time she was twenty. They don't move, don't shift, don't reach back.

panels.narration: She preps the morning intake alone, as she prefers it. The overnight staff have gone. The hallways are white and quiet.

panels.text: The new arrival comes in at 6:14am. Male, mid-twenties, listed as a medical anomaly — found unconscious, no detectable pulse for eleven minutes, then spontaneous revival. The ER didn't know what to do with him, so they sent him here for monitoring.

panels.narration: Ji-ho checks the paperwork without looking up.
panels.speaker: Ji-ho
panels.dialogue: He's alive?

panels.speaker: Orderly
panels.dialogue: Technically, yes. Vitals are stable now. They just need somewhere quiet to keep him until the doctors figure out what happened.

panels.text: Ji-ho nods. Glances toward the room. And stops.

His hands, resting on the blanket, show something she has never seen on a living person — an emotion with no name she has ever learned. Not grief. Not fear. Something like longing, but older. Like missing a place that doesn't exist yet.

panels.speaker: Ji-ho
panels.dialogue: (quietly) What happened to you?

panels.text: In the Sunken Kingdoms, cartographers are the most feared profession. They do not draw what is. They draw what was — and sometimes, what has stopped being true.

panels.narration: Ren is seventeen and still an apprentice, which is embarrassing. Most cartographers earn their stamp by fourteen.
Master Olia doesn't sugarcoat it.
panels.speaker: Master Olia
panels.dialogue: The problem isn't your technique. Your lines are precise. Your scale is faultless. The problem is you keep drawing things that aren't on the source maps.

panels.speaker: Ren
panels.dialogue: But they're there. I can see them when I look at the territory.

panels.speaker: Master Olia
panels.dialogue: You see things that don't exist, and that is not a skill. It is a liability.

panels.text: That evening, Ren is sorting the archive when he finds it — a sealed map case with no label, tucked behind forty years of water-survey scrolls.
Inside is a single map. Clean, precise, expertly drawn. It shows a city Ren has never heard of: Vael's Rest, population 40,000, located three days east of the capital.
The date in the corner reads: Year 14, Post-Sinking. The current year is Year 7.

panels.narration: Ren stares at it for a long time.
panels.speaker: Ren
panels.dialogue: ...This was made seven years from now.
